column4: Question ID (Mandatory and Unique, for example: 1.1, 1.2, Etc)
column5: Question Title (Mandatory)
column6: Question Description (Optional)
column7: Answer Type (Mandatory, one of the following options: 1 for Open Answer, 2 for Dropdown - Single Select, 3 for Dropdown - Single Select - Open answer, 4 for Date, 5 for Dropdown - Multiple Select, 6 for Dropdown - Multiple Select - Open answer)
column8: Dropdown Options (Mandatory if column G is Dropdown. List options for the dropdown separated by a pipe character, for example: Yes|No|Undefined)
column9: DropDown Warnings (Optional, for every option on column H you can define a message, for example: Great|Oh|Think twice)
column10: Dropdown Scoring (Mandatory, set this to 1 if you want to enable scoring for this question. Otherwise set to 0)
column11: Dropdown Option Score (Mandatory if J is set to 1, set the score of the question as an integer positive number, for example 10)
column12: Dropdown Option Score Multiplier (Mandatory if J is set to 1. For every option of the dropdown in column H define an integer positive number that will be multiplied by the value on column K, for example: 1|2|10)
column13: Dropdown Option Show another Question (Optional and only possible if column G is dropdown.For every option in column H you can define one or more question IDs from column D to be shown when the option is selected. For example: 1.2 1.6||6.7 would display questions 1.2 and 1.6 if the first option is selected, none if the second is selected and 6.7 if the third option is selected)
column14: Question Comments & Attachments (Mandatory, select 0 if you do not want to show this option or 1 if you want to let users provide comments and attachments for each question)

column5: Is there a team or individual responsible for endpoint security?
column6: This should include laptops, desktops, mobile phones, tablets, and any other user endpoint that accesses company data.

column5: Provide the names of the team members or individuals responsible for endpoint security.
column6: Include internal and outsourced responsibilities, if applicable.

column5: Which endpoint types are in scope for this assessment? Select one or more than one.
column6: Select every type that can access company systems, email, file storage, VPN, SaaS platforms, or customer data.
column8: Company laptops|Company desktops|Personal laptops|Company mobile phones|Personal mobile phones|Tablets|Shared devices|Other

column5: Do you maintain an inventory of all endpoints that access company systems or data?
column6: This inventory should include laptops, desktops, mobile phones, tablets, and shared devices.

column5: Which tool is used to maintain the endpoint inventory?
column6: Common examples include MDM, EDR, CMDB, directory inventory, or manual asset registers.

column5: Are endpoints assigned to named users or owners?
column6: Shared devices should still have an accountable business owner.

column5: Is there a signed acknowledgement of the responsibilities associated when endpoints are assigned?
Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove endpoint ownership is recorded?
column6: Typical evidence includes inventory exports, MDM records, HR onboarding records, or device assignment logs.

column5: Are company endpoints enrolled into a centralized management tool?
column6: Examples include MDM, UEM, EDR, configuration management, or directory-based device management.

column5: Which tool is used to manage endpoints?
column6: For example, Microsoft Intune, Jamf, Kandji, Workspace ONE, JumpCloud, Google Endpoint Management, or another tool.

column5: Can unmanaged endpoints access company network?
column6: This includes personal devices and devices not enrolled into MDM or EDR.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove unmanaged devices are blocked or restricted?
column6: Auditors typically like to see conditional access policies, MDM compliance rules, access logs, or screenshots of enforcement settings.

column5: Do endpoints require centrally managed or asset owner defined authentication before access is granted to the device?
column6: Authentication should be required after boot, wake, and screen lock.

column5: Is multi-factor authentication required for access to company systems from endpoints?
column6: This should include email, VPN, administrative portals, production systems, and sensitive SaaS applications.

column5: Do endpoints automatically lock after a defined period of inactivity?
column6: The lock period should be defined by policy and technically enforced where possible.

column5: Is full-disk encryption enforced on laptops and desktops?
column6: Examples include BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS, or equivalent encryption technologies.

column5: Is this solution centrally managed or every asset owner must encrypt their devices on their own?
Which encryption technology is used for laptops and desktops?
column6: Include the tool name and whether recovery keys are centrally escrowed.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove endpoint disks are encrypted?
column6: Auditors typically like to see MDM or operating system reports showing encryption status by device.

column5: Is device encryption enforced on mobile phones and tablets that access company data?
column6: This includes company-owned and approved personal mobile devices.

column5: Which tool or setting enforces mobile and tablet encryption?
column6: Common examples include MDM compliance policies or operating system-level encryption settings.

column5: Can company data be remotely wiped from lost, stolen, or terminated-user endpoints?
column6: This should include laptops, mobile phones, and tablets where company data is stored or synchronized.

column5: Are endpoints protected by antivirus, anti-malware, or EDR software?
column6: This should cover laptops, desktops, and any supported servers or shared workstations in scope.

column5: Is this solution centrally managed or every asset owner must encrypt their devices on their own?
Which antivirus, anti-malware, or EDR solution is used?
column6: For example, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Sophos, ESET, or similar tools.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove endpoint protection is deployed and monitored?
column6: Auditors typically like to see deployment coverage reports, alert dashboards, and incident/ticket records.

column5: Are endpoint operating systems patched from a central location or updates are left to the asset owner?
column6: The SLA should define how quickly critical, high, medium, and low severity updates are applied.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove endpoint patch SLAs are met?
column6: Auditors typically like to see patch compliance reports, vulnerability reports, exception approvals, and remediation tickets.

column5: Are mobile phones and tablets allowed to access company data?
column6: Company data includes email, calendars, files, chat, SaaS applications, and customer information.

column5: This includes phones not owned by the organization?
Are mobile devices centrally managed or their controls are up to the device owner?
column6: For example, MDM enrollment, managed applications, containerization, conditional access, or app-level data loss prevention.

column5: Which solution is used to control mobile devices?
Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove mobile and BYOD access is controlled?
column6: Typical evidence includes MDM policies, app protection policies, conditional access rules, and device compliance reports.

column5: Are removable storage devices controlled on endpoints?
column6: Examples include USB storage, external disks, memory cards, and other removable media.

column5: Is this control centrally managed by the organization?
column6: Include whether removable storage is blocked, read-only, encrypted, allowed by exception, or monitored.

column5: Are exceptions for removable storage formally approved and time limited?
column6: This applies when users can request temporary access to USB or other removable media.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove removable media controls are managed?
column6: Auditors typically like to see endpoint policy settings, exception approvals, logs, and periodic reviews.

column5: Can remove devices be wiped up remotely?
column6: The process should cover reporting, access revocation, remote wipe, incident assessment, and evidence collection.

column5: Are endpoints returned, wiped, or disabled during employee offboarding?
column6: Include who must be notified, timelines, and which technical actions are performed.

column5: How broken or end-of-life end-point devices are permanently wiped out?
column6: This should include company laptops, mobile phones, tablets, and access from personal devices where company data exists.

column5: Upload any policy, procedure, standard you have in regards to this topic
